terms.definition: Stands for Electronic Speed Controller. It translates digital signals into motor power, acting like a gas pedal for your drone![cite: 1]
Stands for Flight Controller. This is the computer brain of the drone that helps it balance and fly smoothly.[cite: 1]
Stands for Lithium Polymer battery. It has a high discharge rate chemistry used in drones to give them huge bursts of energy![cite: 1]
Stands for RPM per Volt, which tells you how fast the motor spins. Lower KV is used for higher voltages (6S) or larger props.[cite: 1]
Stands for Video Transmitter. It sends the camera feed to the pilot's goggles so you can see where you are flying.[cite: 1]
Stands for First Person View. This means flying using goggles that show the drone's perspective, like you are a tiny pilot sitting inside![cite: 1]
The tough skeleton of the drone that holds all of your cool electronic parts together safely.
The strong muscles of the drone! They spin super fast to lift your drone into the sky.
The spinning plastic blades that push the air down so your drone can fly up.
The fuel tank! It holds all the electricity the drone needs to wake up and zoom around.
The ears of the drone. It listens carefully to the secret commands from your remote control.
A special invisible bridge that beams live video from your flying drone straight to your screen.
A tough little camera you strap to the top to record awesome, high-quality videos of your adventures.
A camera on a robotic arm that keeps your video super smooth, even when the drone is doing crazy flips!
Bright, colorful flashing lights that make your drone look awesome and help you see it at night.
A space-satellite tool that helps the drone know exactly where it is in the world so it never gets lost.
Special robot eyes that help the drone see walls and trees so it doesn't bump into them.
The remote control you hold in your hands to tell the drone where to go.
The little metal stick on the drone and the remote that helps them talk to each other over long distances.
Any extra weight the drone carries, like a camera, a sensor, or even a small package.
The strong pushing power created by the spinning propellers that makes the drone lift off the ground.
Tilting the drone forward to fly straight ahead, or tilting it backward to fly in reverse.
Tilting the drone sideways so it can slide left or right through the air.
Spinning the drone left or right like a spinning top, without tilting it.
The control stick that gives the motors more juice so the drone can fly faster and higher.
When the drone floats perfectly still in one spot in the air, like a hummingbird.
A special setup step where you teach the drone what is perfectly flat and level.
Secret radio messages the drone sends back to you to tell you how much battery is left and how fast it is going.
A super strong and super light material used to build the drone's frame so it won't break easily in a crash.
Stands for Power Distribution Board. It is like a water pipe system, but it shares electrical juice from the battery to all the parts.
The process of introducing your remote control to your drone so they become best friends and talk only to each other.
Special video glasses you wear to see exactly what the drone's camera sees.
Little metal or plastic pillars that space out the drone's computer boards so they don't touch and short circuit.
Stands for On-Screen Display. These are the helpful words and numbers that pop up on your video screen while flying.
The secret software code that lives inside the drone's brain and tells it how to behave.
A tiny sensor inside the brain that helps the drone feel if it is tilting, so it can correct itself and stay flat.
Another tiny sensor that feels when the drone is moving really fast or falling.
An emergency backup plan! If the drone loses connection with your remote, the failsafe tells it to land safely.
A special, super-fast motor with no rubbing parts inside, making it powerful and long-lasting.
The inside part of the motor that stays completely still and uses magnets to push the spinning part.
The outside bell part of the motor that spins around really fast to turn the propeller.
A fun video game you can play on your computer to practice flying without crashing your real drone!
Stands for Line of Sight. This means you are flying the drone by looking right at it in the sky, instead of using goggles.
The metal and plastic plugs that snap wires together easily.
Melted metal used like super-glue to stick electrical wires safely to the computer boards.
How scooped or twisted the propeller blades are. More scoop means it grabs more air!
Stands for Clockwise. It means a propeller or motor spins to the right, just like the hands on a clock.
Stands for Counter-Clockwise. It means a propeller or motor spins backwards to the left.
A flying machine with exactly four motors and four propellers. The most popular type of drone!
A mega drone with six motors and six propellers instead of four, giving it extra lifting power!
